preFix.title: Finding draughts with a smoke pencil
preFix.subtitle: A room-by-room survey to spot the gaps before you start sealing
preFix.excerpt: A smoke pencil shows where cold air is leaking into a house by making the airflow visible. A methodical survey on a cold, still day covers skirting boards, window edges, loft hatches, electrical sockets, and pipe entries. About half a day's work, and it costs nothing if you have a candle or a stick of incense.
preFix.sourceNotes:
Association for Environment Conscious Building (AECB), Domestic Airtightness guide. https://aecb.net

Passivhaus Trust, airtightness guidance for existing buildings.

Energy Saving Trust, draught-proofing and airtightness resources for homes. https://energysavingtrust.org.uk

content.text: A smoke pencil shows where cold air is leaking into a house by making the airflow visible. On a cold day. Warm air rises and escapes through high gaps, which pulls cold air in through low ones. Hold a lit stick of incense or a smoke pencil near a suspected gap and the smoke will deflect sideways if there is a draught. No special kit needed beyond a candle or incense, a calm hand, and a room-by-room plan.

Half a day is enough to walk through an average house and mark every gap that needs sealing. Smoke makes air infiltration visible. Which is otherwise easy to miss until you live with the cold spot for a winter.
When to do the survey
Outside temperature below 10°C with the heating on. The bigger the gap between indoor and outdoor temperature, the stronger the airflow you can see.
Low wind. A windy day can push smoke in from high gaps and confuse the picture.
Close all windows and external doors before starting. You want the natural pressure difference, not wind through an open door.
Where to check, room by room
Ground floor
Floorboard gaps. Hold the smoke 20 mm above the floor. A draught from a suspended floor usually enters at the perimeter.
Skirting boards. Check with a hand first, then with smoke at the skirting-floor join.
External doors. Including the back door, side gate door, and French doors.
Electrical sockets in external walls. Remove the faceplate and hold smoke at the gap around the back-box. Any gap into the wall cavity is a leak path.
Upper floors and ceilings

Loft hatch edges. One of the largest single leak paths in a typical UK house. Hold smoke at the hatch edge. Check too for warm air going up past the hatch into the loft. Not only cold air coming down.
Recessed ceiling lights. Downlighters that go up into the loft void are a major route for warm air to escape if the loft is not insulated above them.
Window reveal edges. The gap between the window frame and the structural reveal in old timber sash windows is a steady draught path.
Pipe and cable entries
Anywhere a pipe or cable enters an external wall: water supply pipes. Gas supply, TV and telephone cables, boiler flues. All should be sealed with fire-rated sealant or mineral wool packing.
Extractor fan openings without a back-flap. A bathroom extractor with no damper is an open hole when the fan is off.
Recording the results

Sketch a simple floor plan. Or print one from a planning application if you have it, and mark each gap with a number. Write one line per gap: location, rough size, and the fix you plan. The list becomes the draught-sealing work schedule.
After the survey: where to start

The loft hatch, skirting boards. And pipe entries are usually the fastest wins: accessible, cheap to seal. And high-impact because the warm air loss from the top of a house drives the rest of the airflow. Window and door gaps come next. Floorboard gaps come last because they need the floor covering lifted.

For a numeric before-and-after measurement, airtightness testing with a blower door gives a quantified result. It is not needed for a domestic draught-sealing job. But it is the only way to measure improvement objectively; energy assessors offer the service.
Method
Follow the steps below.
